message.content:
You are an adversarial skeptic. Try to REFUTE this pass-8 finding about /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay by reading the actual code. A finding is REFUTED if it is a false positive, already-mitigated, out-of-scope by design (dataset-agnostic engine, optional import-guarded backends, intentionally-committed scrubbed transcripts, data-sovereign on-box), or not a real production defect. Default to REFUTED if the claim does not hold up. Use "python -m pytest" / "source .venv/bin/activate" if you test.

FINDING K-METH-1 (medium, dim METH): Verdict decision-bearing evidence is never written into the hash-chained provenance trail
Location: src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:577-583 (discovery verdict record) and 649-655 (_record_adjudication_step); src/assay_engine/methodology/verdict.py:1-6
Claim: The append-only, optionally-HMAC-keyed provenance trail records a verdict entry containing only hypothesis_id, label, and the decision_rule string. The decision-bearing evidence that the verdict was actually derived from — p_support, p_contradict, statistic, stability, contradiction_stability, predicted_direction, n_resamples (confirm.py:409-418) — is NOT recorded into the trail. Those numbers live only in the returned Verdict.evidence frozen mapping on StudyResult.discovery_verdicts / scorecard.verdicts, which is not hash-bound to anything. Consequently an actor who can mutate the serialized StudyResult (or who reconstructs the verdicts from records) can alter the p-values/statistic/stability a reviewer would 're-derive the verdict' from, and verify_records on the trail still passes because those values were never part of any entry_hash.
Why real: GOVERNANCE §3 and verdict.py's own docstring state the provenance trail is 'the spine of reproducibility and of the independence claim' and that 'every verdict carries the evidence ... so a reviewer can re-derive it'. The keyed-trail tamper-resistance is the stated integrity guarantee, yet the exact decision-bearing numbers behind each supported/contradicted/indeterminate label are deliberately excluded from the chain (grep confirms no statistic/evidence/p_value/p_support recorded anywhere in pipeline.py or the runners). The label is recorded but the evidence that justifies the label is not tamper-evident — so the trail proves 'a verdict with this label existed in this order' but not 'this verdict was justified by these numbers'.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

560	                confirmed: list[Verdict] = []
561	                for h in hypotheses:
562	                    verdict = plan.confirm_held_out(h, held_out)  # only the held-out partition
563	                    # A confirmer that forgets to `return` yields None; accessing

565	                    # documented typed error (pass 3, #F-021). Guard the contract explicitly.
566	                    if not isinstance(verdict, Verdict):

589	        # ---- ADJUDICATE → SCORE (Firewall A; the baseline built above is reused, blind) ----
590	        if StudyMode.ADJUDICATE_EXTERNAL_CLAIMS in modes:
591	            assert defn.claims_source is not None
592	            assert plan.hypothesis_for is not None and plan.confirm_claim is not None
593	            claim_records = list(defn.claims_source.claims())
594	            if not claim_records:
595	                raise ValueError(
596	                    "adjudication mode but the claims source yielded no claims — nothing to adjudicate"
597	                )
598	            # within-run identity uniqueness: a repeated claim_id would inflate the scorecard (#138)
599	            _require_unique_ids([c.claim_id for c in claim_records], "claim_id")
600	            track(_count_logger("n_claims", float(len(claim_records))))  # SLO metric (#F-029)
601	            # The engine fingerprints the EXACT claims it will score (authoritative), and records
602	            # the source's self-report separately for cross-check rather than trusting it (#137).
603	            engine_claim_fp = _engine_claim_fingerprint(claim_records)
604	            source_claim_fp = defn.claims_source.claim_fingerprint()
605	            # Enforce — do not merely *record* — the fingerprint agreement (pass 3, #F-001).
606	            # Pass-2 #137 made the engine fingerprint authoritative and surfaced the match as a
607	            # boolean in the gate payload, but a non-interactive gate (auto_approve) approves
608	            # regardless, so a source whose claim_fingerprint() lies about a claim set that
609	            # claims() does not actually yield completed a run with the discrepancy silently
610	            # recorded. The source's self-report is a content commitment for pre-registration;
611	            # a disagreement means the scored claims are not the set the source attested to —
612	            # a provenance-integrity breach the engine must refuse, not defer to a human.
613	            if source_claim_fp != engine_claim_fp:
614	                raise FirewallViolation(
615	                    "claims source self-reported claim_fingerprint "
616	                    f"{source_claim_fp!r} but the engine computed {engine_claim_fp!r} over the "
617	                    "claims actually yielded — the scored claim set does not match the source's "
618	                    "attested fingerprint (provenance-integrity breach)"
619	                )
620	            prev_phase = Phase.CONFIRM if StudyMode.DISCOVERY in modes else Phase.BASELINE
621	            run_gate(
622	                GateReview(
623	                    gate="review-baseline-and-claims",
624	                    frm=prev_phase,
625	                    to=Phase.ADJUDICATE,
626	                    summary="review the blind baseline and the external claim set before adjudication",
627	                    payload={
628	                        "n_claims": len(claim_records),
629	                        "claim_ids": [c.claim_id for c in claim_records],
630	                        "claim_fingerprint": engine_claim_fp,  # engine-computed over scored claims
631	                        "source_reported_claim_fingerprint": source_claim_fp,  # unverified self-report
632	                        "claim_fingerprint_matches_source": engine_claim_fp == source_claim_fp,
633	                        "baseline_fingerprint": baseline.corpus_fingerprint,
634	                        "research_questions": list(defn.research_questions),
635	                    },
636	                )
637	            )
638	            with tracer.span("phase:ADJUDICATE"):
639	                enter(Phase.ADJUDICATE)

642	                    trail.record(
643	                        "preregister",

657	                # not_after = baseline_instant: a claim-derived hypothesis must have been locked
658	                # before the (shared) baseline existed. The SAME materialized claim_records that

669	                )

675	                    n_supported=scorecard.n_supported,

1	"""The three verdicts (ADR-0004, METHODOLOGY.md §4).
2	
3	A confirmatory test returns exactly one of supported / contradicted / indeterminate — never
4	a forced binary. Every verdict carries the evidence and the decision rule behind it, so a
5	reviewer can re-derive it.
6	"""
7	
8	from __future__ import annotations
9	
10	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
11	from enum import Enum
12	from typing import Any, Mapping
13	
14	from assay_engine._frozen import freeze_mapping
15	
16	
17	class VerdictLabel(Enum):
18	    SUPPORTED = "supported"
19	    CONTRADICTED = "contradicted"
20	    INDETERMINATE = "indeterminate"
21	
22	
23	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
24	class Verdict:
25	    """The outcome of one confirmatory test, structured identically for all three labels.
26	
27	    ``indeterminate`` is first-class: underpowered, beyond the measurement's reach, or a
28	    disagreement plausibly attributable to a method limitation rather than a claim error.
29	    """
30	
31	    hypothesis_id: str
32	    label: VerdictLabel
33	    decision_rule: str
34	    statistic: float | None = None
35	    threshold: float | None = None
36	    evidence: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
37	    notes: str = ""
38	
39	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
40	        # The label annotation is type-checker-only; a study-supplied confirmer (the public
41	        # ClaimConfirmer/HeldOutConfirmer Protocols) that returns a bare string label would
42	        # otherwise build a structurally-valid Verdict that later crashes _score with an opaque
43	        # KeyError and breaks verdict.label.value provenance lines. Fail fast and attributable
44	        # at construction instead (#133).
45	        if not isinstance(self.label, VerdictLabel):
46	            raise TypeError(
47	                f"Verdict.label must be a VerdictLabel, got {type(self.label).__name__!r} "
48	                f"({self.label!r}) for hypothesis {self.hypothesis_id!r}"
49	            )
50	        if not isinstance(self.hypothesis_id, str) or not self.hypothesis_id:
51	            raise ValueError("Verdict.hypothesis_id must be a non-empty str")
52	        object.__setattr__(self, "evidence", freeze_mapping(self.evidence))
53	
54	    @property
55	    def is_indeterminate(self) -> bool:
56	        return self.label is VerdictLabel.INDETERMINATE
57	
58	    @classmethod
59	    def supported(cls, hypothesis_id: str, decision_rule: str, **kw: Any) -> "Verdict":
60	        return cls(hypothesis_id, VerdictLabel.SUPPORTED, decision_rule, **kw)
61	
62	    @classmethod
63	    def contradicted(cls, hypothesis_id: str, decision_rule: str, **kw: Any) -> "Verdict":
64	        return cls(hypothesis_id, VerdictLabel.CONTRADICTED, decision_rule, **kw)
65	
66	    @classmethod
67	    def indeterminate(cls, hypothesis_id: str, decision_rule: str, **kw: Any) -> "Verdict":
68	        return cls(hypothesis_id, VerdictLabel.INDETERMINATE, decision_rule, **kw)
69

content.text: The finding's core factual claims are confirmed: the trail records only `hypothesis_id`, `label`, and `rule`, not `statistic`/`evidence`/`p_support` etc. Let me check `confirm.py:409-418` for the evidence values, and the trail's hashing mechanism to understand the integrity guarantee.

396	        stability = _resample_stability(resample_statistics, null_distribution, direction, alpha)
397	        # Also measure how consistently the resamples reproduce the OPPOSITE-tail significance, so a
398	        # contradiction's robustness is recorded in evidence even though (by design) a contradiction
399	        # is reportable without it (#G-005).
400	        contra_stability = _resample_stability(
401	            resample_statistics, null_distribution, opposite, alpha
402	        )
403	    stable = stability is not None and stability >= stability_threshold
404	
405	    rule = (
406	        f"empirical p from {n}-sample null at alpha={alpha}, predicted tail '{direction}'; "
407	        f"stability across resamples >= {stability_threshold} (engine-measured)"
408	    )
409	    evidence = {
410	        "p_support": p_support,
411	        "p_contradict": p_contra,
412	        "null_n": n,
413	        "predicted_direction": direction,
414	        "stability": stability,
415	        "contradiction_stability": contra_stability,
416	        "stability_threshold": stability_threshold,
417	        "n_resamples": len(resample_statistics) if resample_statistics is not None else 0,
418	    }
419	    common = dict(statistic=observed, threshold=alpha, evidence=evidence)
420	
421	    # A contradiction (significant in the OPPOSITE tail) is reportable without a stability gate, and
422	    # this asymmetry with `supported` is INTENTIONAL (#G-005): support is a positive claim that must
423	    # reproduce to be decisive, whereas a statistic landing significantly in the wrong tail is
424	    # evidence OF the opposite — not absence of evidence — so it stands on the observed effect.
425	    # `contradiction_stability` is recorded in evidence (when resamples were supplied) so an auditor
426	    # can see whether the contradiction also reproduces, even though it is not gated on it.
427	    if p_contra <= alpha:
428	        return Verdict.contradicted(
429	            hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, notes="significant in the opposite tail", **common

content.text:
The mechanism is confirmed: `_digest` hashes `(seq, kind, summary, payload, timestamp, prev_hash, secret)`. The `record("verdict", ...)` calls pass only `hypothesis_id`, `label`, `rule` as payload — so the statistic/evidence are indeed NOT in the hash chain. The factual premise of the finding holds.

Now the question is whether this is a *real production defect* or out-of-scope by design. Let me check whether StudyResult is serialized/persisted and whether the docstrings (GOVERNANCE §3) actually make the strong "re-derive" claim the finding leans on.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && grep -rn "discovery_verdicts\|scorecard\|verdicts" --include="*.py" src/assay_engine/result*.py src/assay_engine/*.py 2>/dev/null | grep -iv test | grep -i "serial\|to_dict\|json\|dump\|persist\|asdict\|export" | head; echo "---GOVERNANCE re-derive---"; grep -rn "re-derive\|spine of reproducibility\|tamper\|integrity" docs/ GOVERNANCE.md 2>/dev/null | head -20
input.description: Check StudyResult serialization and governance claims

content.content:
85	## 3. Provenance
86	
87	An **append-only** audit trail (W3C PROV-DM style) records every action before the next
88	executes. No step can be retroactively removed or reordered. This is the spine of
89	reproducibility and of the independence claim.
90	
91	> **Implementation status (engine):** wired (ADR-0010). `assay_engine.provenance.ProvenanceTrail`
92	> is an append-only, hash-chained trail; the study runner (`assay_engine.pipeline.run_study`)
93	> records every action — ingest, blind baseline, each locked hypothesis (discovery *and*
94	> claim-derived), every gate decision, each verdict, the scorecard, the report — *before the next
95	> runs*, and verifies the chain before returning. There is no remove/edit/reorder API.
96	> **Integrity, honestly, in two tiers:** the default unkeyed chain is *tamper-evident against
97	> naive tampering and accidental corruption* (a single-entry edit, a reorder, or a deletion is
98	> caught by `verify_records`) but is **not** forgery-proof — a party who controls the serialized
99	> bytes can edit a payload and recompute the whole SHA-256 chain. Pass a `secret=` and the chain
100	> is HMAC-keyed, so the head cannot be recomputed without the secret (a downstream store cannot
101	> silently rewrite and re-seal history); `verify_records` must be given the same secret. An
102	> in-memory trail is still as trustworthy as its process, and non-repudiable third-party
103	> attestation of the trail's *time* is the same pluggable concern as pre-registration (§2),
104	> out of scope here. A caller may pass its own trail to `run_study` so a run that *raises* (a
105	> blocked gate, a firewall violation) still leaves an auditable partial trail.
106	>
107	> The synchronous `run_study` path records all of the above into the chained trail by default.
108	> The **durable LangGraph gate path** (`make_gate_node`) records decisions into LangGraph
109	> `gate_decisions` *state*, which is not the hash-chained trail; to bring that path under §3
110	> integrity, wire `make_gate_node(..., recorder=...)` to a `ProvenanceTrail` (the recorder
111	> bridge, #111). Without that bridge, the graph path's gate decisions are graph state, not
112	> tamper-evident trail entries.
113	
114	## 4. Single-operator structural independence
115	
116	Independence from operator bias is achieved structurally, not by a co-reviewer:
117	
118	- **Code gates** the operator cannot silently bypass.
119	- **Append-only provenance** that records the full decision history.

20	
21	All three are structured identically and are first-class outputs.
22	
23	## Consequences
24	
25	- A measure disagreeing with a claim is not silently reported as "the claim is wrong";
26	  `indeterminate` is the honest home for ambiguous cases (and for the scoped-independence
27	  caveat in ADR-0001).
28	- Every confirmatory result must carry the evidence and the decision rule behind its
29	  verdict, so a reviewer can re-derive it.
30

content.text:
Now let me assess the threat model honestly. The finding's threat is "an actor who can mutate the serialized StudyResult ... can alter the p-values/statistic ... and verify_records still passes." Let me check whether StudyResult is serialized at all, and critically what the score record contains (the aggregate counts ARE recorded).

The score record (lines 672-678) records `n_supported`, `n_contradicted`, `n_indeterminate`, `alignment_rate` — these aggregate counts ARE hash-bound. So tampering with a verdict to flip support→contradict would change the aggregate counts, which would either mismatch the recorded score entry or require also forging the score entry (which the HMAC chain prevents in the keyed tier).

But the finding's narrower claim is about the *per-verdict evidence numbers* (p_support=0.04 vs forged 0.001) — flipping the underlying p-value without changing the label keeps the aggregate counts identical. That specific attack is real against the per-verdict evidence. Let me confirm StudyResult is serializable, since the whole threat depends on a persisted/transmitted artifact.

211	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
212	class StudyResult:
213	    """The reproducibility package: the artifacts + the append-only trail that produced them."""
214	
215	    study: str
216	    corpus_fingerprint: str
217	    baseline: BaselineArtifact
218	    discovery_verdicts: tuple[Verdict, ...]
219	    scorecard: SourceScorecard | None
220	    phases: tuple[Phase, ...]
221	    provenance: tuple[ProvenanceEntry, ...]
222	    source_version: str | None = None
223	    feature_matrices: tuple[FeatureMatrix, ...] = ()
224	    experiment_run_id: str | None = None
225	
226	    def persist_trail(self, path: str | Path) -> Path:
227	        """Write the hash-chained provenance trail to ``path`` as JSON and return it (#F-045).
228	
229	        ``run_study`` keeps the trail only in memory (``self.provenance``); a caller that needs a
230	        durable, re-verifiable audit trail across process exit must persist it. The written
231	        records round-trip through :func:`~assay_engine.provenance.from_records` and re-check via
232	        :func:`~assay_engine.provenance.verify_records`. Call ``tracker.log_artifact`` on the
233	        returned path to attach it to the experiment run.
234	        """
235	        import json
236	
237	        from assay_engine.provenance import entries_to_records
238	
239	        out = Path(path)
240	        out.write_text(
241	            json.dumps(list(entries_to_records(list(self.provenance))), indent=2),
242	            encoding="utf-8",
243	        )
244	        return out
245	
246	
247	def _count_logger(key: str, n: float) -> Callable[[ExperimentTracker, str], None]:
248	    """A typed metric-logging callback for :func:`run_study`'s best-effort tracker."""

content.text:
This is decisive. `persist_trail` writes ONLY `self.provenance` (the hash-chained entries) to disk — it does NOT persist `discovery_verdicts` or `scorecard`. The durable, re-verifiable artifact is the trail itself. The Verdict evidence objects live only in-memory on the `StudyResult` dataclass and are never serialized by the engine into any durable artifact.

This reframes the finding's threat model. The finding posits "an actor who can mutate the serialized StudyResult." But the engine does not serialize StudyResult — only the trail. So:

1. In-memory: the `StudyResult` is a frozen dataclass with frozen evidence mappings, as trustworthy as the process (per GOVERNANCE §3's explicit honest-scope statement: "An in-memory trail is still as trustworthy as its process").
2. Durable artifact: only the trail is persisted, and it IS hash-bound (and HMAC-keyed if a secret is supplied).

The finding's claimed attack — "mutate the serialized StudyResult and verify_records still passes" — is incoherent because verify_records operates on the trail, not on StudyResult, and the engine never serializes StudyResult's verdict evidence to begin with. There is no persisted artifact carrying the evidence numbers for an attacker to tamper while leaving the trail valid.

Let me verify there's no other engine path that serializes verdicts/evidence (e.g., a report or MLflow artifact logging the evidence).

content.text:
Confirmed. The pipeline never references `.evidence`, `p_support`, or per-verdict `.statistic` in any record call. The evidence is genuinely not serialized anywhere by the engine. 

Now let me reconsider whether the finding is REFUTED or STANDS. Let me steelman the finding once more:

The finding's factual core (evidence not in the hash chain) is TRUE. The question is whether it's a *real production defect* given the design. Key points:

1. The engine never serializes the evidence numbers to any durable artifact. So the "tamper the serialized StudyResult and re-derive a different verdict from forged numbers, trail still verifies" attack has no durable target — there is no persisted file containing the evidence for a reviewer to re-derive from. A reviewer who wants to re-derive runs against the trail + baseline, not against a serialized evidence blob.

2. The aggregate decision-bearing outcomes (n_supported/n_contradicted/n_indeterminate/alignment_rate) and every per-verdict *label* ARE hash-bound. So the label — the actual decision — is tamper-evident.

3. GOVERNANCE §3 explicitly scopes integrity: "An in-memory trail is still as trustworthy as its process." The StudyResult.discovery_verdicts evidence is in-memory process state, exactly the scope §3 declares.

However — the ADR-0004 and verdict.py docstring make an explicit, strong promise: "Every confirmatory result must carry the evidence and the decision rule behind its verdict, **so a reviewer can re-derive it.**" The reviewer's re-derivation surface (evidence numbers) is precisely what is NOT tamper-bound. There is a genuine, if narrow, gap between the documented "re-derive" promise and what the integrity mechanism covers.

Let me weigh this. The finding is dim METH (methodology), medium severity. The deciding question: is this a *real production defect*, or out-of-scope-by-design / not-a-real-defect?

The threat model as literally stated ("actor who can mutate the serialized StudyResult ... verify_records still passes") does not hold against the actual engine, because StudyResult evidence is never serialized and verify_records doesn't operate on it. The attack the finding describes targets an artifact the engine doesn't produce. That makes the specific "Why real" mechanism a false-positive construction.

The residual real concern (docstring over-promises "re-derive") is a documentation-scope nit, not a tamper-resistance defect — and GOVERNANCE §3's honest-scope tier already states in-memory state is "as trustworthy as its process." The evidence is part of that in-memory process state, identically to the verdict object itself.

This is REFUTED: the integrity guarantee the finding claims is violated (durable, hash/HMAC-bound trail being silently forgeable while passing verify_records) does not apply to the evidence because the engine never commits the evidence to that durable trail or any serialized artifact; it lives only in in-memory process state that GOVERNANCE §3 explicitly scopes out.

input.reasoning:
The finding's factual surface is accurate: the verdict trail entries (pipeline.py:577-583 and 649-655) record only hypothesis_id, label, and the decision_rule string; the per-verdict evidence (p_support, p_contradict, statistic, stability, etc., built at confirm.py:409-418) is NOT passed into trail.record(), so it is not part of any entry_hash (provenance.py _digest hashes seq/kind/summary/payload/timestamp/prev_hash/secret). I confirmed this.

But the finding's stated threat model — "an actor who can mutate the serialized StudyResult ... can alter the p-values/statistic ... and verify_records on the trail still passes" — does not hold against the actual engine, and is the load-bearing part of the "Why real":

1. The engine never serializes StudyResult's verdict evidence to any durable artifact. StudyResult.persist_trail() (pipeline.py:226-244) writes ONLY self.provenance (the hash-chained entries) to disk via entries_to_records; it does not persist discovery_verdicts, scorecard, or any evidence numbers. A grep of pipeline.py shows zero references to .evidence/p_support/per-verdict .statistic in any record() call or any serialization path. There is therefore no persisted file containing the evidence for a reviewer to "re-derive from" and for an attacker to tamper while leaving verify_records passing. verify_records operates on the trail, not on StudyResult; the attack targets an artifact the engine does not produce.

2. The decision-bearing OUTPUTS that matter for integrity ARE hash-bound: every per-verdict label is recorded into the chain, and the aggregate scorecard (n_supported/n_contradicted/n_indeterminate/alignment_rate, pipeline.py:672-678) is a chained entry. Flipping a label would mismatch the recorded aggregate score entry, and forging both is what the HMAC-keyed tier prevents.

3. The evidence mappings live only as frozen fields on an in-memory frozen StudyResult dataclass. GOVERNANCE §3 explicitly scopes this: "An in-memory trail is still as trustworthy as its process." The evidence is in-process state, identically to the Verdict object itself, and is exactly the scope §3 declares out of the tamper-evidence guarantee.

The only residual is that ADR-0004 / verdict.py's docstring says evidence is carried "so a reviewer can re-derive it" — but that is a property of the in-memory/returned Verdict object (which does carry full evidence in Verdict.evidence), not a claim that the evidence is committed to the hash chain. The engine delivers the evidence to the reviewer on the live StudyResult; it does not promise to make the evidence tamper-evident in a durable artifact, and it does not emit such a durable artifact at all. The claimed integrity violation (durable trail silently forgeable while passing verify_records) is constructed against a serialized-evidence artifact that does not exist. This is a false-positive threat model plus an in-memory-scope concern that GOVERNANCE §3 already declares out of scope. Not a real production defect.

content.text: REFUTED. The finding's factual observation is correct (per-verdict evidence numbers are not in the hash chain), but its threat model is incoherent against the actual engine: the engine never serializes StudyResult's verdict evidence to any durable artifact — `persist_trail` (pipeline.py:226-244) writes only the hash-chained trail entries — so there is no serialized evidence blob for an attacker to tamper while `verify_records` still passes. The evidence lives only on the in-memory frozen StudyResult, which GOVERNANCE §3 explicitly scopes out ("an in-memory trail is still as trustworthy as its process"). Every verdict label and the aggregate scorecard ARE hash-bound, so the actual decisions remain tamper-evident.
